title: H-Ai-Nikr-Agi.psm1 BOM corruption: leading 0x3F byte causes '?#' parse failure
description: H-Ai-Nikr-Agi.psm1 had a corrupted BOM where byte 0 was 0x3F ('?') instead of 0xEF (the first byte of the UTF-8 BOM EF BB BF). The original bytes were 3F 23 20 56... instead of EF BB BF 23 20 56.... PowerShell saw '?# VersionTag' producing the error "The term '?#' is not recognized as a name of a cmdlet". This is the same 0x3F corruption pattern previously seen in AssistedSASC.psm1 and PwShGUI-IntegrityCore.psm1 during the 2026-04-04 encoding fix session, where BOM repair left 0x3F byte artifacts.
resolution: Stripped leading 0x3F byte, prepended correct UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF). Verified: B0=0xEF B1=0xBB B2=0xBF B3=0x23 (correct). PSParser reports 0 errors. File size 24048 bytes.
detection_method: User-reported '?#' error at module import. Confirmed by [IO.File]::ReadAllBytes() showing byte 0 = 0x3F. Same pattern as P023 double-encoding repair artifacts.
root_cause: During the 2026-04-04 encoding remediation session, the BOM repair process for double-encoded files sometimes replaced the first BOM byte (0xEF) with 0x3F while leaving the remaining BOM bytes (0xBB 0xBF) intact. This produces a valid-looking but unparseable file since 0x3F is the '?' character.
exception_message: The term '?#' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program.
preventionRule:
1. After BOM repair, always verify first 3 bytes are EF BB BF followed by the expected first content byte (usually 0x23 for '#').
2. Check for 0x3F at position 0 as part of post-repair validation.
3. Add byte-level verification to the SIN remedy engine for P023 fixes.
4. Never assume BOM repair succeeded without reading back the bytes.
